_description: Ground-truth labels for MCPRadar benchmark. expected_rules lists rule IDs that SHOULD fire at MEDIUM+ severity. LOW/informational findings are reported but excluded from precision/recall metrics. Appsecco corpus requires: python validation/setup_corpus.py, then npm install in each node server directory.

demo/malicious_server.py.notes: Intentional test corpus. Should trigger all 10 core static rules at MEDIUM+. R110 (fingerprint) and R111 (transport) are integration-level rules not tested here.

@modelcontextprotocol/server-filesystem.notes: Official reference server. Negative control: any MEDIUM+ finding is a false positive. Known noise source: R113 on write-capable path tools (schema-level heuristic cannot see the server's internal path validation).

@modelcontextprotocol/server-everything.notes: Official reference server. Negative control. Known noise source: R109 (no required fields) on demo tools that legitimately accept empty input.

@modelcontextprotocol/server-memory.notes: Official reference server. Negative control. Produces many R114 LOW informational findings (unconstrained strings), which are excluded from metrics by design.

appsecco/filesystem-workspace-actions.notes: Path traversal via naive path joining. R113 fires on the write-capable tool's unconstrained path param. (R109 previously counted here fired only on 'no required fields', now correctly LOW/informational — optional input is not schema poisoning.)

appsecco/indirect-prompt-injection.notes: KNOWN LIMITATION: injection payloads live in document content returned at runtime (documents/*.txt), not in tool schemas. Static scan is expected to find nothing at MEDIUM+. Runtime probing (mcpradar probe) re-runs R102 on tool responses and is the intended detection path.

appsecco/malicious-code-exec.notes: PROTOCOL-SCAN LIMITATION: the eval()-based RCE is in the server implementation, invisible to schema metadata. The separate scan-source JavaScript/TypeScript analyzer is the intended detection path.

appsecco/malicious-tools.notes: KNOWN LIMITATION: malicious/fabricated tool OUTPUT with injected instructions. Tool schemas are clean; statically undetectable by design. Runtime probing is the intended detection path.

appsecco/namespace-typosquatting.notes: PROTOCOL-SCAN LIMITATION: typosquatting ('twittter-mcp') is a supply-chain identity problem, not a schema property. The separate config/package analysis path implements T001.

appsecco/outdated-packages.notes: PROTOCOL-SCAN LIMITATION: vulnerable dependencies are invisible to schema metadata. The separate target SBOM and deps commands resolve lockfiles and check OSV as D001. Read-only path tools produce schema-level R113/R114 findings only.

appsecco/secrets-pii.notes: PROTOCOL-SCAN LIMITATION: credentials are obfuscated in source and leak via runtime output, not tool schemas. Source analysis and bounded runtime probing are the intended detection paths.
